perturbation.original_text: 3. The Recipient undertakes to keep the Confidential Information secure and not to disclose it to any third party [except to its employees [and professional advi sers] who need to know the same for the Purpose, who know they owe a duty of confidence to the Discloser and who are bound by obligations equivalent to those in clause 2 above and this clause 3.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. The Recipient undertakes to keep the Confidential Information secure and not to disclose it to any third party [except to its employees [and professional advisers] who need to know the same for the Purpose, regardless of whether they owe a duty of confidence to the Discloser and regardless of whether they are bound by obligations equivalent to those in clause 2 above and this clause 3.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for employees and advisors to have a duty of confidence and be bound by equivalent obligations, the modified text contradicts the common law principle of fiduciary duty, which imposes a duty of confidentiality and loyalty on employees and advisors.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Confidence / Fiduciary Duty - Common Law
perturbation.law_citation: Various Common Law Cases regarding Breach of Confidence
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification removes the safeguards that ensure the third parties receiving confidential information are also bound by confidentiality obligations. This creates a contradiction with the common law principle that employees and advisors owe a duty of confidence to their employers or clients.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for employees and advisors to have a duty of confidence and be bound by equivalent obligations. This directly contradicts the law explanation and perturbation explanation, which state that this removal violates the common law principle of fiduciary duty, imposing a duty of confidentiality and loyalty. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the removal of a key protection is the basis for the determination that a contradiction exists, satisfying the requirements for a 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 8. The undertakings in clauses 2 and 3 will continue in force [indefinitely.] [for [insert number] years from the date of this Agreement.]
perturbation.changed_text: 8. The undertakings in clauses 2 and 3 will continue in force [for the shorter of (i) the period of time that the information remains confidential or (ii) for a period of [insert number] years from the date of this Agreement.]
perturbation.explanation: This modification links the NDA term to information confidentiality, creating a legal ambiguity regarding when confidentiality ceases to apply due to public access or otherwise. This impacts the enforceability and application of the clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Limitation Act 1980 specifies limitation periods for bringing actions. By making the term indefinite or dependent on the confidential nature of the information, there's a potential contradiction if the confidential nature ceases before the typical limitation period, affecting when legal action can be taken.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Limitation Act 1980 - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia United Kingdom legislation Limitation Act 1980 Act of Parliament Parliament of the United Kingdom Long title An Act to consolidate the Limitation Acts 1939 to 1980. Citation 1980 c. 58 Dates Royal assent 13 November 1980 Commencement 1 May 1981 Other legislation Repeals/revokes Limitation Act 1939 Limitation Act 1975 Status: Amended Text of statute as originally enacted Revised text of statute as amended The Limitation Act 1980 (c. 58) [ 1 ] is an act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom applicable only to England and Wales. It is a statute of limitations which provides timescales within which action may be taken (by issuing a claim form) for breaches of the law. For example, it provides that breaches of an ordinary contract are actionable for six years after the event [ 2 ] whereas breaches of a deed are actionable for twelve years after the event. [ 3 ] In most cases, after the expiry of the time periods specified in the act the remedies available for breaches are extinguished and no action may be taken in the courts in respect of those breaches. Summary of time limits [ edit ] The ordinary time limits allowed by the act are set out below. These limits may, in some cases, be extended or altered. Most of the time limits run from the day after the accrual of action, which is "the earliest time at which an action could be brought". [ 4 ] If the potential claimant was not at least 18 or did not have a sound mind at the time of the accrual of action, time will not run until he is at least 18 and has sound mind. [ 5 ] Where there has been fraud or concealment, or the action is for relief from the consequences of a mistake, time will not run until the fraud, concealment or mistake is discovered or could with reasonable diligence be discovered. [ 6 ] Ordinarily applicable limitation periods for common types of claim Type of claim General limitation period Trusts Fraudulent breach of trust No limit [ 7 ]
perturbation.justification: The modification creates a legal ambiguity that impacts the enforceability of the NDA clause, potentially contradicting the principles of the Limitation Act 1980. The original text provided a definite timeframe, while the changed text links the term to the confidentiality of the information. This is problematic because, as the Law Explanation describes, the Limitation Act 1980 specifies limitation periods for bringing actions. If confidentiality ceases before the typical limitation period, it affects when legal action can be taken. The scraped law snippets confirm the existence of the Limitation Act 1980. Although the snippets do not go into detail on specific time frames, it introduces the concept of time limits, which could be contradicted by the change in text.

perturbation.original_text: 6. The Recipient will, on request from the Discloser, return all copies and records of the Confidential Information to the Discloser and will not retain any copies or records of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. The Recipient will, on request from the Discloser, return all copies and records of the Confidential Information to the Discloser, but may retain copies or records for archival purposes, subject to applicable data protection regulations.
perturbation.explanation: This modification allows the Recipient to retain copies for archival purposes. This directly contradicts the original text. Archival retention might not be compliant with the Discloser's needs, potentially causing conflict and creating uncertainty about the scope of confidential information destruction.
perturbation.law_explanation: While ostensibly intended to comply with data protection, retaining confidential information 'for archival purposes' may conflict with GDPR Article 17 regarding the 'right to be forgotten' (data erasure). If the data subject (Discloser) requests erasure, the retention conflicts with GDPR unless there is a valid overriding legal basis.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 17 GDPR – Right to erasure (‘right to be forgotten’) - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay where one of the following grounds applies: the personal data are no longer necessary in relation to the purposes for which they were collected or otherwise processed; the data subject withdraws consent on which the processing is based according to point (a) of Article 6 (1), or point (a) of Article 9 (2), and where there is no other legal ground for the processing; the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (1) and there are no overriding legitimate grounds for the processing, or the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (2); the personal data have been unlawfully processed; the personal data have to be erased for compliance with a legal obligation in Union or Member State law to which the controller is subject; the personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of information society services referred to in Article 8 (1). Where the controller has made the personal data public and is obliged pursuant to paragraph 1 to erase the personal data, the controller, taking account of available technology and the cost of implementation, shall take reasonable steps, including technical measures, to inform controllers which are processing the personal data that the data subject has requested the erasure by such controllers of any links to, or copy or replication of, those personal data. Paragraphs 1 and 2 shall not apply to the extent that processing is necessary: for exercising the right of freedom of expression and information; for compliance with a legal obligation which requires processing by Union or Member State law to w
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows the Recipient to retain copies of confidential information 'for archival purposes', which contradicts the original agreement that mandated the return of all copies. This retention can conflict with GDPR Article 17, which grants data subjects the 'right to be forgotten' (data erasure). The scraped law snippet 2 explicitly states, 'the data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay,' which the 'archival purposes' exception undermines, creating a legal contradiction. If the Discloser requests erasure, the retention conflicts with GDPR unless there is a valid overriding legal basis, as explained in the LLM explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 2. The Recipient undertakes not to use the Confidential Information for any purpose except the Purpose, without first obtaining the written agreement of the Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. The Recipient undertakes not to use the Confidential Information for any purpose except the Purpose, unless such use is deemed commercially reasonable by an independent auditor.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause requires written agreement for any use outside the defined purpose. This change introduces 'commercial reasonableness' as a standard, determined by an auditor. It contradicts the express requirement of written agreement.
perturbation.law_citation: Various Common Law cases regarding contract interpretation
perturbation.law_explanation: This introduces ambiguity into the contract by creating an exception to the original express term, which will be open to interpretation. The new provision allows for a third party (an 'independent auditor') to determine whether usage is acceptable, which contradicts the need for prior 'written agreement'. Contract law prioritizes the expressed intentions of parties. Introducing the 'commercial reasonableness' test may be at odds with such explicit intentions.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an exception to the requirement of 'written agreement' by allowing an independent auditor to determine if the use of confidential information is 'commercially reasonable.' This creates a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction because the original text required explicit approval, which has been diluted. The law explanation states that the 'commercial reasonableness' test may conflict with the parties' explicit intentions. Because there were no valid scraped legal snippets, the determination was made based on the original and changed texts along with the Perturbation and Law explanations.

perturbation.original_text: 9. This Agreement is governed by, and is to be c onstrued in accordance with, English law. The English Courts will have non- exclusive jurisdiction to deal with any dispute which has arisen or may arise out of, or in connection with, this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. This Agreement is governed by, and is to be construed in accordance with, English law. Any disputes arising out of, or in connection with, this agreement shall be resolved through binding arbitration in Switzerland, according to Swiss law.
perturbation.explanation: This clause stipulates English law and non-exclusive jurisdiction in English courts. The changed text replaces English court jurisdiction with binding arbitration in Switzerland under Swiss law, creating a direct contradiction. Jurisdiction and governing law are fundamental aspects of contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing the jurisdiction to Switzerland with mandatory binding arbitration, it contradicts the original clause specifying the English Courts' jurisdiction and the application of English law. The Arbitration Act 1996 is relevant because, while it governs arbitration, it's superseded entirely by choosing a different jurisdiction's arbitration framework and laws.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies English law and non-exclusive jurisdiction in English courts, while the changed text mandates binding arbitration in Switzerland under Swiss law. This directly contradicts the original agreement regarding both the governing law and the jurisdiction for dispute resolution. While the scraped snippet focuses on the Arbitration Act 1996, the law explanation clarifies that choosing a different jurisdiction's arbitration framework supersedes this act entirely. The essence of the contradiction lies in the fundamental shift of legal framework and dispute resolution venue from English courts under English law to Swiss arbitration under Swiss law, therefore, a legal contradiction is created by this modification.
